name: Gui
description: Gui (鬼) is a general term in Chinese mythology for a demon, ghost, or spirit. Unlike benevolent deities, Gui are typically malevolent or at least mischievous spirits of the deceased. They are often depicted as tormented beings, suffering from their earthly sins or attachments. The concept of Gui is deeply rooted in Chinese ancestor worship and the belief in an afterlife where the actions of the living can affect the deceased. Different types of Gui exist, ranging from hungry ghosts (餓鬼 èguǐ), perpetually starved due to their greed in life, to vengeful spirits seeking retribution for wrongs suffered. They are often associated with darkness, graveyards, and abandoned places. Rituals and offerings are performed to appease them and prevent them from causing harm to the living. The fear of Gui is a significant cultural influence, shaping traditions and beliefs related to death, funerals, and ancestor veneration. Their appearance varies greatly, often reflecting the cause of their death or their dominant vice in life, and they are believed to possess a range of supernatural abilities, including shapeshifting, possession, and the ability to inflict illness and misfortune. The Gui represent the consequences of negative actions and the importance of living a virtuous life to avoid a similar fate in the afterlife. They are a constant reminder of the interconnectedness between the living and the dead in Chinese cosmology.
subtitle: Restless spirits of the deceased
appearance: The appearance of a Gui varies greatly. Some appear as grotesque, decaying corpses, while others retain a semblance of their former selves. They may have pale skin, sunken eyes, and long, unkempt hair. Some are depicted with animalistic features, reflecting their sins or the circumstances of their death. Hungry ghosts are often portrayed with bloated bellies and thin necks, unable to satisfy their insatiable hunger.
habitat: Gui are typically found in places associated with death and decay, such as graveyards, abandoned buildings, dark forests, and battlefields. They are also believed to roam the human realm, seeking out opportunities to cause mischief or seek revenge. Some Gui are said to reside in the underworld, where they are subjected to various forms of punishment for their transgressions.
behavior: Gui are generally malevolent or mischievous. They may haunt the living, causing illness, misfortune, or even death. Some seek revenge for wrongs suffered in life, while others are simply driven by insatiable hunger or a desire to return to the world of the living. Rituals and offerings are performed to appease them and prevent them from causing harm.

keyMyths.title: The Hungry Ghost Festival
keyMyths.description: The Hungry Ghost Festival is a time when the gates of hell are believed to open, allowing Gui to roam the earth. Offerings and performances are made to appease the spirits and prevent them from causing harm.

keyMyths.title: Zhong Kui the Demon Queller
keyMyths.description: Zhong Kui is a mythical figure who is said to be able to vanquish Gui. He is often depicted in Chinese art and folklore as a fierce warrior with the ability to control and defeat evil spirits.

sources.author: Werner, E.T.C.
sources.description: A comprehensive collection of Chinese myths and legends.
sources.text: A comprehensive collection of Chinese myths and legends.
